message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-8 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. A finding is REFUTED if it is a false positive, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, data-sovereign on-box), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if the claim does not hold up. Use "python -m pytest" / "source .venv/bin/activate" if you test.

FINDING K-METH-2 (medium, dim METH): Standalone adjudicate() does not enforce the claim-set fingerprint commitment that run_study enforces
Location: src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:103-181 (adjudicate); contrast pipeline.py:603-619; commitment documented at contracts/claims.py:75-84
Claim: claims.py documents claim_fingerprint() as 'an enforced commitment, not an unverified label' that the engine recomputes over the materialized claims and 'raises FirewallViolation if the source's self-report disagrees'. That enforcement exists ONLY in run_study (pipeline.py:603-619). The public adjudicate() runner materializes claims_source.claims() (line 165) but never calls claims_source.claim_fingerprint() and never cross-checks it (grep confirms no fingerprint reference in adjudication.py). A study that drives the documented standalone adjudicate() entry point with a claims source whose claim_fingerprint() attests to one set while claims() yields another completes adjudication with no detection — the content commitment the docs promise is silently absent on this path.
Why real: adjudicate() is a documented public composition entry point (its docstring describes 'the full adjudication, enforcing Firewall A by construction'). The fingerprint commitment is described in claims.py as engine-enforced provenance integrity, but that text is only true for run_study. A caller reasonably relying on the documented guarantee gets no fingerprint cross-check, so a source can attest to one claim set and adjudicate another — the exact provenance-integrity breach #F-001 was created to close, left open on the non-pipeline path.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

103	def adjudicate(
104	    corpus: Corpus,
105	    claims_source: ExternalClaimsSource,
106	    *,
107	    baseline_builder: BaselineBuilder,
108	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
109	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
110	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
111	    source_name: str = "external",
112	) -> tuple[BaselineArtifact, SourceScorecard]:
113	    """Run the full adjudication, enforcing Firewall A by construction.
114	
115	    1. The claims source is kept in this function's local scope and is NOT handed to the
116	       builder; the baseline is built inside a sealed :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` that holds
117	       nothing. This is a **signature-level** guarantee: the builder is never *handed* a
118	       claims source by the engine, so it cannot accidentally consult the claims. It is NOT
119	       frame isolation — a builder that deliberately reflects into this runner's call stack
120	       (``sys._getframe``) could reach ``claims_source``; preventing that would require
121	       running the builder in a separate process, which is out of scope. The guarantee is
122	       against *accidental* circularity, not against a builder that willfully defeats the
123	       firewall.
124	    2. After the baseline exists, each claim is mapped (by ``hypothesis_for``) to its typed,
125	       pre-stated ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` hypothesis whose ``source_claim_id`` must match the claim
126	       and which must be genuinely **pre-registered** — :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.
127	       preregistration.require_preregistered` checks (via the supplied ``authority``) that the
128	       proof binds this hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes the
129	       **baseline build** (the ordering instant is captured before the build). A claim-derived
130	       hypothesis must therefore be locked *before* ``adjudicate`` is called — ``hypothesis_for``
131	       looks up an already-locked hypothesis; locking on demand inside it (after the baseline
132	       exists) is rejected, so the hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline.
133	    3. The verdict's ``hypothesis_id`` must match the hypothesis it answers (no misattribution),
134	       and the verdicts are aggregated into a :class:`SourceScorecard`.
135	
136	    ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
137	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
138	    adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default, so a study must supply a real one.
139	
140	    Returns ``(baseline, scorecard)``. Raises ``FirewallViolation`` (incl.
141	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.PreRegistrationError`) on any
142	    claim↔hypothesis↔verdict identity mismatch, a non-``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` (data-surfaced)
143	    hypothesis, or a hypothesis that is not verifiably pre-registered before confirmation.
144	    """
145	    # The claims source stays in THIS function's local scope and is never given to the builder.
146	    # The builder receives a guard holding nothing (a blind-mode signal only), so there is no
147	    # object reachable from the builder that contains the claims — not even via the guard's
148	    # internals. This is stronger than a custodial guard, whose private `_claims_source` a
149	    # determined builder could read past the sealed `release()` check (adversarial review).
150	    # Captured BEFORE the baseline is built: an EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis derives from the
151	    # external claim, not from the baseline, so it must be pre-registered before the baseline (the
152	    # answer) exists. Using this as `not_after` makes "lock before the result is in hand" real —
153	    # a hypothesis locked on demand inside `hypothesis_for` (i.e. after the build) is rejected,
154	    # forcing studies to pre-lock their claim-derived hypotheses (#83).
155	    not_after = _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
156	
157	    guard: ClaimBlindGuard[ExternalClaimsSource] = ClaimBlindGuard()
158	    with guard.sealed():
159	        baseline = baseline_builder.build(corpus, claim_guard=guard)
160	
161	    # Materialize and guard empty here (pass 3, #F-010): the pipeline runner rejects an empty
162	    # claim set as a misconfiguration, but the standalone entry point silently returned a vacuous
163	    # total=0 scorecard — an inconsistent contract where one caller errors and the other does
164	    # not. Fail loud uniformly: adjudicating zero claims scores nothing and is never intended.
165	    materialized = list(claims_source.claims())
166	    if not materialized:
167	        raise ValueError(
168	            "adjudication requires at least one claim; the claims source yielded none "
169	            "(nothing to adjudicate)"
170	        )
171	
172	    scorecard = adjudicate_with_baseline(
173	        baseline,
174	        materialized,
175	        hypothesis_for=hypothesis_for,
176	        confirm=confirm,
177	        authority=authority,
178	        not_after=not_after,
179	        source_name=source_name,
180	    )
181	    return baseline, scorecard
182	
183	
184	def adjudicate_with_baseline(
185	    baseline: BaselineArtifact,
186	    claims: "Iterable[ClaimRecord]",
187	    *,
188	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
189	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
190	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
191	    not_after: _dt.datetime,
192	    source_name: str = "external",
193	    on_step: "Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None" = None,
194	) -> SourceScorecard:
195	    """Adjudicate a **materialized claim set** against an already-built blind baseline (core).
196	
197	    Factored out of :func:`adjudicate` so a composed pipeline that builds the baseline once
198	    (and shares it with discovery) can reuse the identical, hardened Firewall-A loop instead of
199	    rebuilding the baseline. The caller is responsible for having built ``baseline`` blind and

Let me check its docstring claims and how it's exposed/documented.

content.text: `adjudicate` is exported in `methodology/__init__.py` as public API. Let me check the ADR for the firewall to see whether the fingerprint commitment is described as a property of `adjudicate()` specifically, or only of `run_study`.

input.description: Inspect ADR-0008 for scope of fingerprint guarantee

content.content:
15:keep the claims out of that step, then adjudicate. Nothing in the engine forced that ordering.
27:1. Provide `methodology.adjudicate(corpus, claims_source, *, baseline_builder, hypothesis_for,
45:3. It enforces that every adjudicated claim yields an `EXTERNAL_CLAIM` (pre-stated) hypothesis,

25	## Decision
26	
27	1. Provide `methodology.adjudicate(corpus, claims_source, *, baseline_builder, hypothesis_for,
28	   confirm)` as the engine-owned adjudication composition. It enforces Firewall A at the
29	   **signature level**: the claims source is kept in the runner's local scope and is *never
30	   handed to the builder* (whose `build(corpus, *, claim_guard)` signature has nowhere to
31	   receive it), and the baseline is built inside a sealed `ClaimBlindGuard` that holds nothing.
32	   So the builder cannot *accidentally* consult the claims — the realistic failure mode (a
33	   study glancing at the claims "just for features"). Honest scope: this is **not** frame
34	   isolation. A builder that deliberately reflects into the runner's call stack
35	   (`sys._getframe`) could still reach the local `claims_source`; only running the builder in a
36	   separate process would prevent that, which is out of scope. The guarantee is against
37	   *accidental* circularity, not a builder that willfully defeats the firewall. (An adversarial
38	   review found both this limit and that an earlier custodial-guard design was weaker still —
39	   its private `_claims_source` could be read past the sealed check; holding nothing is the
40	   better of the in-process options.)
41	2. The runner enforces claim↔hypothesis↔verdict **identity**: the hypothesis's
42	   `source_claim_id` must match the claim, the hypothesis must be locked (pre-registered), and
43	   the verdict's `hypothesis_id` must match the hypothesis — so a `hypothesis_for` or `confirm`
44	   bug cannot silently misattribute a verdict and pollute the scorecard.
45	3. It enforces that every adjudicated claim yields an `EXTERNAL_CLAIM` (pre-stated) hypothesis,
46	   not a data-surfaced one — conflating the two would breach the discover/confirm separation.
47	4. It implements §5 as a `SourceScorecard`: counts of supported/contradicted/indeterminate and
48	   an `alignment_rate` = supported / (supported + contradicted) — the frequency with which the
49	   independent baseline corroborates the source on *decisive* claims, with indeterminate
50	   excluded. This is a measured frequency, explicitly **not** a normative judgement of the
51	   source; richer dimensions (where it aligns, diverges, leaves gaps) are data-surfaced
52	   downstream, per §5, not a fixed checklist.
53	
54	The study still supplies the `confirm` callable (only it knows the baseline's structure and how
55	a claim maps onto a measurement) and the `hypothesis_for` adapter (claim → typed hypothesis).
56	The engine owns the *ordering and the firewall*, the study owns the *domain*.
57	
58	## Consequences
59

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. The ADR (the authoritative design doc for this firewall) defines `adjudicate()` as the engine-owned composition and enumerates the guarantees it enforces (items 1-4): signature-level Firewall A, identity matching, EXTERNAL_CLAIM enforcement, scorecard. The fingerprint commitment is notably NOT in this list of `adjudicate()` guarantees.

But the finding's core claim rests on `claims.py:75-84`, which states the fingerprint is "an enforced commitment" that "the engine recomputes... and raises FirewallViolation if the source's self-report disagrees." Let me verify whether that docstring scopes the guarantee to a particular path, and confirm the docstring of `adjudicate()` itself does not promise the fingerprint check.

The `adjudicate()` docstring (lines 113-143, read earlier) enumerates exactly three enforcement steps (Firewall A signature, pre-registration, identity) — it does NOT mention fingerprint at all. So `adjudicate()` does not over-promise. The over-promise is in `claims.py:75-84` which says "the engine recomputes this... and raises FirewallViolation" unconditionally, attributing it to "the engine" generically (it cites #F-001, which lives in pipeline.py).

Let me confirm the F-001 pass-3 record to understand the intended scope.

35	
36	### Methodology + pipeline integrity (batch 1)
37	- **F-001** enforce claim-fingerprint agreement (was recorded, not enforced; `auto_approve`
38	  laundered a lying source). New public `claim_set_fingerprint()` is the canonical scheme.
39	  *(partial-closure of pass-2 #137)*
40	- **F-002** validate `predicted_direction` at construction + defensively in `confirm_unit_level`
41	  *(partial-closure of pass-2 #128)*
42	- **F-006** terminal deterministic `run_end` provenance entry

content.text:
I now have enough to adjudicate. Let me assess.

The finding's factual core is verified and correct:
- `adjudicate()` (adjudication.py:103-181) materializes `claims_source.claims()` at line 165 but never calls `claim_fingerprint()` — grep confirms zero fingerprint references in the file.
- Fingerprint enforcement (recompute + `FirewallViolation` on mismatch) exists ONLY in `pipeline.py:603-619`.
- `adjudicate` IS a public, documented entry point (exported in `methodology/__init__.py:17,57`; first-class item in ADR-0008).
- `claims.py:75-84` documents the fingerprint as "an enforced commitment, not an unverified label" that "the engine recomputes... and raises FirewallViolation if the source's self-report disagrees."

Now the skeptic test — is there a legitimate basis to call this REFUTED?

1. Does `adjudicate()` over-promise? Its own docstring enumerates exactly three enforcement steps and never mentions fingerprint. So the docstring of the entry point itself does not promise the check. The original F-001 was explicitly scoped to the `auto_approve` gate-laundering path, which is a pipeline-only construct (`adjudicate()` has no gate). The fingerprint commitment was designed as a pipeline/pre-registration provenance feature.

2. However, `claims.py:75-84` makes an unscoped, contract-level promise attributing the recompute-and-enforce behavior to "the engine" — and `ExternalClaimsSource` is the shared contract used by BOTH paths. A study author implementing `claim_fingerprint()` reads this contract and is told the engine enforces it. On the `adjudicate()` path it does not. So there is a genuine doc/code divergence.

But the substantive security question: is this a real provenance breach left open? The fingerprint is a *self-report by the same source object* that yields `claims()`. The engine's authoritative artifact (pipeline) recomputes over the actual scored claims. On the `adjudicate()` path, the scorecard is computed over `materialized = claims_source.claims()` — i.e., the actually-scored claims. There is no separate fingerprint persisted into a provenance artifact by `adjudicate()` (it returns `(baseline, scorecard)`, no fingerprint field). So there is no artifact in which a "lying" fingerprint could be laundered — the gap F-001 closed (a recorded-but-false fingerprint in the run artifact / auto_approve gate) does not exist on the `adjudicate()` path because `adjudicate()` neither records nor consumes the fingerprint at all.

The finding's harm theory ("a source can attest to one claim set and adjudicate another") is hollow: `adjudicate()` scores exactly what `claims()` yields and emits no fingerprint attestation. There is no second set, no recorded commitment, nothing downstream relies on the unmentioned fingerprint. The breach F-001 fixed was specifically the *recording* of an unverified fingerprint as provenance under auto_approve — that recording does not happen here.

This is at most a documentation imprecision in `claims.py` (attributing to "the engine" generically what only the pipeline does), not a production provenance defect on the `adjudicate()` path. The entry point's own docstring is accurate. REFUTED.

input.reasoning:
The finding's literal facts are correct but its harm theory does not hold, so it is not a real production defect on the adjudicate() path.

Verified facts: adjudicate() (src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py:103-181) materializes claims_source.claims() at line 165 and never calls claim_fingerprint() (grep finds zero fingerprint references in adjudication.py). The recompute-and-enforce check (FirewallViolation on mismatch) exists only in pipeline.py:603-619. adjudicate is a public, documented entry point (exported in methodology/__init__.py:17,57; first-class in ADR-0008).

Why REFUTED:
1. adjudicate()'s OWN docstring (lines 113-143) enumerates exactly three enforced guarantees — signature-level Firewall A, claim<->hypothesis<->verdict identity + pre-registration, and EXTERNAL_CLAIM typing — and never mentions the fingerprint. ADR-0008 (the authoritative design doc) likewise lists the adjudicate() guarantees (items 1-4) and does not include the fingerprint. So the entry point does not over-promise the check.
2. The unscoped "the engine recomputes... raises FirewallViolation" wording lives in claims.py:75-84 (the ExternalClaimsSource contract), which is at most a documentation imprecision attributing to "the engine" generically what only the pipeline does. It is not a code defect.
3. Critically, the breach F-001 actually closed was the RECORDING of an unverified/lying fingerprint into the run's provenance artifact under a non-interactive auto_approve gate (PASS-3.md:37-39). adjudicate() has no gate, records no fingerprint, and consumes no fingerprint: it returns (baseline, scorecard) where the scorecard is computed over `materialized = claims_source.claims()` — i.e., exactly the claims that are scored. There is no separate attested set and no persisted commitment that a false fingerprint could launder. The finding's claimed harm ("attest to one set and adjudicate another") is hollow because the fingerprint is never consulted, recorded, or relied upon on this path — there is nothing to disagree with.

Thus the provenance-integrity breach F-001 was created to close (a recorded false fingerprint laundered through auto_approve) structurally cannot occur on the adjudicate() path. The only residual is a too-broad sentence in the claims.py contract docstring, which is a doc-tightening nit, not a production provenance defect. Out-of-scope/not-a-real-defect -> REFUTED.

content.text: Verdict provided: REFUTED. The finding's harm theory does not hold because `adjudicate()` neither records nor consumes the fingerprint — it scores exactly what `claims()` yields, so the recorded-false-fingerprint laundering breach F-001 closed structurally cannot occur on this path. The only residual is an over-broad sentence in the `claims.py` contract docstring (a doc nit), not a production defect.
